engineering.translation: The place reached by the sāṅkhyas (the renunciates of action) is reached also by the yogis (the practitioners of disciplined action). The one who sees sāṅkhya and yoga as one (ekam) sees clearly. The verse extends 5.4: the destination-claim is symmetric. The engineer who has cultivated mastery through years of architectural reading-and-strategy reaches the same end as the engineer who has cultivated mastery through years of shipping-and-iteration. Both arrive at the operational disposition of the senior — the one whose response is uniform across the visibility gradient, whose review-depth is invariant under business-importance, whose interior posture is decoupled from the artifact. The verse refuses to rank the paths at the level of destination. The clarity (paśyati) the verse names is in seeing the destination as one — not in seeing the paths as identical.
engineering.concrete_scenario: Two senior engineers compare careers at an industry conference. One spent fifteen years deeply on architectural strategy: design docs, RFCs, RFC reviews, architecture-review committees, very few merges per year. The other spent fifteen years deeply on shipping: migrations, hotfixes, on-call rotations, hundreds of PRs per quarter. They sit in the conference hallway and discover, mid-conversation, that their systems-thinking converges. The architect reads a Datadog dashboard the way the shipper does; the shipper reads a system-design diagram the way the architect does. Each has reached the same operational disposition — the senior's interior posture — through opposite surface-paths. Neither path was wrong. Each ranks the other path's external work as different from their own; both recognise the destination as the same.
engineering.falsifiability: The analog fails if a reader concludes that any path can replace any other. The verse's claim is destination-equivalence at a particular level (the realised seer's disposition); it is not a claim that an engineer can substitute one career-shape for another and arrive somewhere. The destination is reached only by deep cultivation in *some* path.
engineering.counter_example: When neither path has been cultivated deeply — when the engineer dabbles in both surface forms without ever building real mastery in either — the verse's destination-equivalence does not apply. The destination is reached *through* the path, not by avoiding both. The shallow-on-both engineer has not arrived anywhere.
engineering.implication: When evaluating two senior engineers from very different career paths, look at the destination they have reached, not the surface of the path. The seer's disposition is the readable signature.
engineering.quotable_line: Both paths reach the same destination; the path's surface differs, the senior's interior posture does not.
